text: The surgeon begins the procedure by taking down the gastrohepatic omentum and identifying the right crus of the diaphragm. The phrenoesophageal membrane is dissected, and the left crus of the diaphragm is separated from the stomach. The gastrophrenic ligament is transected, creating a window in the retrogastric space for a Penrose drain. A myotomy is initiated at the distal gastroesophageal junction, lifting muscle fibers to create a space between the longitudinal circular muscle fibers and the stomach mucosa. The myotomy is extended onto the esophagus in small bites, using the bulging mucosa as a guide.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully transect muscle fibers at the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring the scalpel tip is cooled periodically to prevent mucosal injury. Muscle fibers are stretched and lifted off the mucosa, with small bites taken to complete the myotomy. The myotomy is extended into the mediastinum, with the anterior vagus nerve identified and protected. A measuring ruler confirms the myotomy extends seven to eight centimeters.

The surgeon performs a door fundoplication, starting with sutures from the stomach to the left crus and the divided muscle fibers of the myotomy on the left side. Additional sutures are placed to keep the myotomy open and exposed. The stomach is then rolled onto the right side over the esophagus, with sutures securing it to the right muscle fibers and right crus, completing the fundoplication.
